That's right are the two words that immediately transform any negotiation. Why? What's so special about that's right? What's special is that's right simultaneously signifies that you've created a subtly epiphany in the mind of your counterpart. An enlightening moment that they didn't quite see before. That's right is what people say when they believe they've just heard something that is indisputable. That's right is also confirmation from them that they feel empathy from you. This is a powerful feeling of connection to you specifically. For example, whenever a politician you side with says something that you feel strongly about, you point your finger at the TV and you say that's right. When the that's right moment happens, it would not only be invisible to your counterpart, but it is an indicator the trust-based influence bridge has been built. There's a big difference between that's right and your right. If you fall into the subtle death trap of hearing your right as a response from your counterpart, the conversation is off track. You are in the danger zone. This is where you've sought to be understood before showing understanding. Your right is the worst form of false agreement there is. It's even worse than the counterfeit yes. It's the black hole of negotiations. When people hear your right, they happily stop talking and go away. It's what we all say to people we want to stop talking and drop the issue at hand. We use this with people we genuinely want or feel we are obligated to maintain a relationship with. Do you know who the greatest practitioners of your right are? Spouses. Generally during a conversation that is non-collaborative. So how do you get to a that's right? Summarize your counterpart's arguments. All of your counterpart's feelings about that argument. It's especially critical you give voice to the negative feelings that seem counter to your position. Especially if their assessments seem unfair. Be fearless. Do this with no agreement or disagreement. No denials. Remember the simple formula. Paraphrase plus extensive labeling equals summary. But let's break down the labeling a little bit more. Extensive labeling refers to a series of verbal observations strung together. All of which are based on the information you have gathered during the interaction. This is a deep dive on both the positives and the negatives. While we advocate to never let negative stand in your way, the positives go deep also. To the higher purposes people have, their deeper meanings and their whys. For example, a doctor feels responsibility for not just his or her patient's health, but their lives. They're responsible for providing the extra moments they can give to people's families. Like having a loved one around for another holiday or birthday. A business person wants to make good decisions for their business. They help people have livelihoods and feed their families. They enable people to send their children to college or provide a service the business community needs to thrive. What you're doing is repeating what the world looks like according to your counterpart. When you summarize correctly and you do get a that's right, it will break down the barriers that we're blocking progress. That's right is the best phrase you can get your counterpart to utter. It accelerates the transition from developing a relationship into a place where you have built trust based influence. Influence in its most durable form.
